title: Data Manipulation Recognition Track — Senior
description: A 10-exercise senior recognition track. Read contracts and proposed changes for downstream consumer impact, diagnose multi-team architectural risk, evaluate migration proposals against binding constraints, judge cross-team equivalence (bug vs business-rule), and conduct cross-team incident postmortems with timelines + log excerpts. Five modules: DMS1 Contracts · DMS2 Multi-Team Architecture · DMS3 Migration Decisions · DMS4 Materialization Boundaries · DMS5 Incident Postmortems.

datasets.description: NordGrid meter readings (20-row reference). Senior scenarios reference 50M-meter platform scale on top of the small CSV.

tasks.title: The Dedup Rule No One Wrote Down

description.context: The NordGrid Customer team has owned the silver_meters_dedup pipeline for two years. The pipeline reads Bronze meter readings and emits Silver — one row per (meter_id, date), deduplicated when the upstream system emits two readings for the same key. The current implementation uses `df.sort_values('arrival_ts').groupby(['meter_id','date']).first()`. A senior engineer on the Customer team reviews the code and notes that the dedup logic is order-dependent: the kept row is whichever one arrived first. They open a pull request to replace the idiom with `df.loc[df.groupby(['meter_id','date'])['daily_kw'].idxmax()]` — the value-based dedup pattern from the Mid-Level recognition material. The change is locally correct and the team approves it.
description.task: Read the contract, the proposed change, and the consumer manifest — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.title: Production constraint
evidence.content: The Billing team's invoice pipeline consumes silver_meters_dedup nightly and bills each customer based on the daily_kw value found there. The Billing team's contract with the Customer team is documented at the schema level only — column names and dtypes — but the Billing team's auditor has signed off on the assumption that 'the kept reading is the FIRST one received from the meter for that day, even if a later re-read corrects the value upward'. The auditor's reasoning: 'first reading is the contemporaneous reading, the corrected re-read is a back-dated adjustment that should be tracked separately'.

evidence.title: Platform context
evidence.content: Five teams interact with silver_meters_dedup. The Customer team OWNS it. The Billing team CONSUMES it for invoicing (the auditor-signed-off contract). The Analytics team CONSUMES it for dashboards (no documented contract; assumes daily_kw is 'the meter's reading for that day' without specifying which reading on duplicate days). The Substations team CONSUMES it for capacity reports (assumes the value is comparable across days, no concern about which-row-on-duplicates). The Platform team OWNS the upstream Bronze pipeline that produces the duplicates.

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.caption: 3 rows showing the dedup case for one customer on one day. The 09:14 reading arrived first; the 14:32 re-read arrived later with a corrected (higher) value.

evidence.title: Contract document
evidence.content:
silver_meters_dedup — Schema Contract v2.1 (signed 2024-Q1, last reviewed 2025-Q4):
  - meter_id: string, NOT NULL
  - date: date, NOT NULL
  - daily_kw: float, NULLABLE
  - arrival_ts: timestamp, NOT NULL
  - Primary key: (meter_id, date)
  - Freshness: T+1 by 06:00
  Notes section (free-form, not formally part of the contract): 'Deduplication preserves one row per (meter_id, date). Implementation detail: see customer-team source.'

evidence.label: Proposed change — before
evidence.content: df.sort_values('arrival_ts').groupby(['meter_id','date']).first()

evidence.label: Proposed change — after
evidence.content: df.loc[df.groupby(['meter_id','date'])['daily_kw'].idxmax()]

evidence.title: Change justification
evidence.content: From PR description: 'The current dedup is order-dependent — fragile across upstream-ordering changes. The new form embeds the value rule in the code, removing the ordering dependency. No semantic change for our pipeline.'

evidence.title: Consumer manifest
rows: daily_kw is the FIRST reading received that day (auditor-signed-off)
daily_kw is 'the' reading for that day; ambiguous on which row when duplicates exist
daily_kw values are comparable day-to-day; no concern on per-day dedup choice

fields.label: Q1 — Which downstream consumer's contract is violated by the proposed change?
options: The Analytics team's — their dashboard reads daily_kw without documenting which-row-on-duplicates, and the change shifts the value silently for any meter that had a same-day re-read with a higher correction value

The Billing team's — the auditor-signed-off implicit assumption is 'first reading' and the proposed change picks 'max-value reading' for the same key, which differs whenever a same-day re-read produced a higher corrected value

The Substations team's — their capacity report aggregates daily_kw across many meters and the change shifts the per-meter value by a small amount, which compounds in the aggregate to a measurable shift in the report's total

The Platform team's — they own the upstream Bronze pipeline that emits the duplicates, and the proposed change places more demand on Bronze duplicate-detection logic that the Platform team did not budget for
fields.correctAnswer: The Billing team's — the auditor-signed-off implicit assumption is 'first reading' and the proposed change picks 'max-value reading' for the same key, which differs whenever a same-day re-read produced a higher corrected value
fields.rationale:
Of the four consumers, only one has an implicit assumption specific enough to be violated by the change: the rule that 'first reading received that day' is what gets billed, which Billing's regulator confirmed in writing. The new idiom picks max-value, not first-arrival. Whenever a same-day re-read produced a higher corrected value (the typical case for upward corrections), the kept row changes. Billing's invoice changes by the difference. The Analytics team (option A) has an under-specified assumption ('the reading') that is technically met by either dedup rule — their assumption is too vague to be VIOLATED by the change, even though it is also too vague to be safe. The Substations team (option C) does not depend on which-row-on-duplicates because their report is comparative across days, not absolute. The Platform team (option D) owns Bronze, not Silver consumption, and the change does not affect their pipeline.

When this answer is wrong: If the Billing team's auditor had instead signed off on 'final corrected value' (the alternative auditing convention used by some regulators), the proposed change would actually MATCH their assumption and option B would be wrong. The exercise specifies the auditor signed off on FIRST reading — which is the case where the change violates rather than aligns with the contract.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Conflates 'assumption is vague' with 'assumption is violated'. The Analytics assumption is too vague to be either met or violated specifically — the Billing assumption is specific enough to be violated.

Option C: Reaches for the option that involves multiple meters and aggregation, but the Substations report does not depend on which row is kept per-meter.

Option D: Identifies a real platform concern (Bronze duplicate-detection) but the proposed change is downstream of Bronze and does not affect the Platform team.

fields.label: Q2 — Why did the Customer team's PR review miss the violation?
options: The PR description correctly described the change as 'no semantic change for our pipeline' — the Customer team's pipeline really does not change semantically; the violation is in another team's contract that the Customer team did not look at

The Customer team's senior engineer applied a Mid-Level-correct idiom rule (value-based over order-based dedup) without checking whether the existing order-based dedup encoded an implicit downstream contract; the rule is correct in isolation, wrong in context

The team verified semantic equivalence within their own pipeline (correctly) and treated the order-based idiom as 'fragile' (correctly in the abstract) without recognizing that fragility was itself the contract Billing's auditor depended on — two correct reasoning steps reinforcing into false safety

The Customer team's CI tests all passed, including a regression test that compared the old and new outputs on a sample dataset; the sample dataset happened not to contain any same-day re-read with a higher correction, so the divergence was masked
fields.correctAnswer: The team verified semantic equivalence within their own pipeline (correctly) and treated the order-based idiom as 'fragile' (correctly in the abstract) without recognizing that fragility was itself the contract Billing's auditor depended on — two correct reasoning steps reinforcing into false safety
fields.rationale:
Both halves of the review failure are present and they reinforce each other. The Customer team correctly verified that their own pipeline produced semantically equivalent output for their own purposes (no behavioral change visible inside the Customer team's own tests and metrics). They also correctly applied the abstract rule that 'order-based dedup is fragile, value-based is more robust' — a rule the Mid-Level recognition track teaches explicitly. What they missed is that fragility WAS the contract — the Billing team's auditor signed off precisely because the dedup rule was 'first arrival', a property that the order-based idiom guaranteed and the value-based idiom does not. Option A captures one half (semantic equivalence within team), option B captures the other (rule applied without context), option C names the interaction. Option D's CI-coverage explanation is plausible but less likely as the dominant cause — the test coverage gap is a symptom, not the root cause.

When this answer is wrong: If the team's senior engineer had explicitly considered downstream consumers but concluded (incorrectly) that the change was safe, option B alone would be the right diagnosis — a recognition error in isolation. The exercise's premise (the senior engineer applied a rule from training material without checking context) makes the dual failure pattern dominant.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Names one half of the failure correctly but stops there.

Option B: Names the other half but misses the within-team semantic-equivalence verification that reinforced the false sense of safety.

Option D: Identifies a real test-coverage gap but treats it as the root cause rather than a symptom of the upstream review failure.

fields.label: Q3 — What contract clause should be added to silver_meters_dedup's Schema Contract to prevent the next violation of this type?
options: Add a 'dedup-rule' clause naming the specific rule (e.g., 'first-arrival by arrival_ts ASC') and require any change to the rule to be a versioned contract change reviewed by all consumers, not a Customer-team-only PR review

Add a 'tiebreak-monitoring' clause requiring the Customer team to publish a monthly report of how many (meter_id, date) pairs had multiple input rows and what fraction the dedup rule kept versus dropped, so consumers can verify their assumption against actual data

Add a 'consumer-registration' clause requiring every downstream pipeline to register an explicit assumption statement that the Customer team must consult before any pipeline change, regardless of whether the change appears semantically neutral

Add a 'breaking-change-window' clause requiring a 30-day notice period before any pipeline change ships, giving downstream consumers time to evaluate the change against their own contracts before the change reaches production
fields.correctAnswer: Add a 'dedup-rule' clause naming the specific rule (e.g., 'first-arrival by arrival_ts ASC') and require any change to the rule to be a versioned contract change reviewed by all consumers, not a Customer-team-only PR review
fields.rationale:
Naming the dedup rule explicitly in the contract is the architecturally correct fix. The contract's current 'Notes section' acknowledges deduplication exists but defers the rule to 'see customer-team source' — which means the rule is implicit and not part of the contract. Promoting the rule to a contract clause makes any change to it a versioned contract change requiring consumer review. Option B's monitoring clause is useful but does not prevent the ship — it would surface the change AFTER it shipped. Option C's consumer-registration clause is heavy-handed (every consumer must register every assumption, on every pipeline) and impractical at platform scale. Option D's notice period is a process control rather than a contract clause; it slows the cycle without preventing the misunderstanding that the dedup rule is changeable without consumer review.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms with very high consumer churn (hundreds of consumers, frequent additions), the consumer-registration approach (option C) is sometimes argued as the only scalable answer — though most senior practitioners reject it as too costly. The exercise targets a 5-team platform where the named-rule clause (option A) is unambiguously the right answer.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Confuses 'detection' with 'prevention'. Monitoring detects post-hoc; the contract clause prevents up-front.

Option C: Over-engineers the solution. The asymmetry is the OWNER's responsibility to name the rule; the CONSUMER's responsibility is to read the contract.

Option D: Mistakes process for contract. A 30-day notice slows changes; a contract clause makes them require a different review path.

fields.label: Q4 — Suppose the Customer team must ship the new idiom for an unrelated reason (e.g., the order-based form is genuinely incorrect on a different upstream-ordering pathology that has been observed). What architectural pattern preserves the Billing team's auditor-signed-off behavior without forcing the Customer team to keep the order-based idiom?
options: Maintain two Silver tables — silver_meters_dedup (with the new idiom, value-based) and silver_meters_dedup_first_arrival (the legacy idiom, preserved for the Billing team's auditor-signed-off behavior) — with a contract clause naming which consumer reads from which

Add a daily_kw_first_arrival column to silver_meters_dedup that preserves the auditor-signed-off first-arrival value alongside the new daily_kw column based on the value-based idiom; consumers including Billing pick the column matching their contract

Put the dedup decision in a configuration parameter and let each consumer (Billing for the auditor-signed-off rule, others for the new idiom) specify which dedup variant they want at read time, with the Customer team pre-computing both

Keep silver_meters_dedup with the new value-based idiom and move the first-arrival logic into the Billing team's own pipeline as a downstream re-dedup step that the Billing team owns and tests against their auditor's contract
fields.correctAnswer: Keep silver_meters_dedup with the new value-based idiom and move the first-arrival logic into the Billing team's own pipeline as a downstream re-dedup step that the Billing team owns and tests against their auditor's contract
fields.rationale:
Decoupling the consumer-specific re-dedup back to its consumer is the architecturally cleanest answer. The Billing team OWNS the auditor-signed-off contract; therefore the Billing team should OWN the data-manipulation logic that produces auditor-correct output. The Customer team's pipeline produces a generic Silver table; the Billing team applies their specific re-dedup as a downstream step that they test against their auditor's contract. This decouples the two teams' concerns — Customer team owns Silver semantics, Billing team owns Billing semantics. Option A creates a maintenance burden on the Customer team for a contract that is not theirs. Option B couples the schemas — every consumer now sees both columns whether they care or not. Option C is heavyweight (read-time configurability adds platform complexity for a single consumer's need). The senior pattern: when a downstream consumer has a specific contract, the downstream team owns the logic that produces contract-compliant output, not the upstream team.

When this answer is wrong: If the auditor's contract were a regulatory requirement that affected ALL consumers (e.g., a privacy regulation requiring first-arrival dedup across the platform), option A's separate-table approach becomes correct because the contract is platform-wide rather than consumer-specific. The exercise frames the auditor's sign-off as Billing-specific, which makes consumer-side ownership the right pattern.

Operational note: The platform-level pattern is 'consumer-side ownership of consumer-specific contract logic'. When a downstream consumer has a contract clause that is specific to their use case, they own the data-manipulation step that produces compliance — even if that means re-running a similar transformation that an upstream pipeline also performs. This pattern decouples upstream genericness from downstream specificity and prevents the cross-team contract drift this exercise illustrates. NordGrid's data platform team adopted this rule after a similar incident in 2025 Q2.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Places the maintenance burden on the wrong team. The Customer team would have to maintain two Silver tables for one consumer's contract.

Option B: Couples schemas across consumers. Every Silver reader sees both columns, expanding the schema for one team's need.

Option C: Adds platform complexity (read-time configuration) for a single consumer's specific need.

tasks.title: The Null-Handling Convention That Drifted

description.context: Three regional Analytics teams (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST) have independently consumed the same silver_meters table for years, each applying its own null-handling for daily_kw on read. NORTH drops null rows, SOUTH fills them with zero, EAST fills them with the previous day's value. A new platform initiative consolidates the three teams' work into a shared Gold table gold_regional_summary, owned by the Platform team. The Platform team's senior architect chooses 'fill nulls with zero' as the consolidated convention because it is 'the most defensible default — the data engineer's null is the auditor's missing reading, and zero is the auditor's missing-reading default'. The change is announced. The student must identify which two teams' downstream reports silently change and why.
description.task: Read the contract, the proposed change, and the consumer manifest — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.title: Production constraint
evidence.content: Each team's downstream reports have been audited and signed off under the team's own null-handling convention. The Platform team's choice of 'fill with zero' becomes binding for all three after consolidation. No one team holds veto power; the Platform team's architect makes the call.

evidence.title: Platform context
evidence.content: NORTH Analytics OWNS the NORTH-region monthly capacity report, audited by Region NORTH's grid regulator under the convention 'meter readings with no recorded value are excluded from the regional capacity total'. SOUTH Analytics OWNS the SOUTH-region monthly billing-baseline report, audited under 'meter readings with no recorded value are treated as zero consumption for billing-baseline purposes'. EAST Analytics OWNS the EAST-region monthly demand-forecast report, audited under 'meter readings with no recorded value are filled with the prior day's reading to preserve the time-series continuity required for the forecast model'. The Platform team OWNS the consolidated gold_regional_summary; before consolidation, it owns nothing in this domain.

evidence.title: Input sample
evidence.caption: 3 representative meters across one week. M-A: one null day. M-B: three consecutive null days. M-C: nulls on irregular days. Each team's convention produces a different per-meter monthly total.

evidence.title: Contract document
evidence.content:
Existing per-team contracts (each maintained by the consuming team, NOT centrally documented):
  NORTH: 'daily_kw nulls are excluded from regional capacity totals'
  SOUTH: 'daily_kw nulls are treated as zero consumption for billing baseline'
  EAST: 'daily_kw nulls are forward-filled from the prior day for time-series continuity'
New Gold contract (proposed by Platform team's senior architect):
  'gold_regional_summary applies fill-with-zero to all daily_kw nulls before aggregation. This convention is selected as the most defensible auditor default and binds all three regions uniformly.'

evidence.label: Proposed change — before
evidence.content: Each team applies its own null convention on read from silver_meters

evidence.label: Proposed change — after
evidence.content: Platform team applies fill-with-zero to silver_meters → gold_regional_summary; all three teams read from the consolidated Gold table

evidence.title: Change justification
evidence.content: From the architect's design doc: 'Three different conventions on the same underlying data is a maintenance burden and an audit-trail nightmare. Fill-with-zero is the most defensible default. The change is announced; teams adapt their downstream reports.'

fields.label: Q1 — Which two teams' reports silently change after the consolidation?
options: NORTH and SOUTH — both teams previously had conventions distinct from the new platform default, while EAST's convention happens to coincide with the proposed fill-with-zero policy in the boundary case where there is no previous day to fill from

NORTH and EAST — NORTH's drop-nulls produces a different denominator than fill-with-zero (rows excluded vs rows included as zero), and EAST's forward-fill produces non-zero values for null days (whereas the new policy uses zero) so both regions' totals shift

SOUTH and EAST — SOUTH's old fill-with-zero convention gets re-applied at the new Gold layer creating double-fill arithmetic, and EAST's forward-fill is suppressed by the new policy; only NORTH's drop-nulls happens to align with the post-consolidation behavior on the regulator's audit metric

All three teams change — every team's existing convention is incompatible with the new Gold-layer fill-with-zero policy, regardless of whether the team's old convention happened to produce the same numeric output for some specific dataset on a specific run
fields.correctAnswer: NORTH and EAST — NORTH's drop-nulls produces a different denominator than fill-with-zero (rows excluded vs rows included as zero), and EAST's forward-fill produces non-zero values for null days (whereas the new policy uses zero) so both regions' totals shift
fields.rationale:
Two of the three regions experience a numeric change. NORTH's old drop-nulls convention produced totals over only-recorded days; the new fill-with-zero counts null days as zero-kw rows in the total — same numerator, different denominator (the row count grows), changing the per-day-mean derivative metrics that NORTH's report computes alongside the total. EAST's old forward-fill produced non-zero values for null days; the new fill-with-zero replaces those with zero, dramatically reducing EAST's monthly totals on customers with consecutive null streaks (M-B's case: forward-fill total 4250 vs fill-with-zero total 1720 — a 60% drop). SOUTH was already fill-with-zero, so SOUTH's totals are unchanged numerically — though SOUTH should still be informed of the change. Option A misses EAST. Option C invents a 'double-fill' that does not happen — applying fill-with-zero twice produces the same result as applying it once. Option D over-claims by saying all three change; SOUTH's numbers genuinely do not change.

When this answer is wrong: If SOUTH's convention had been documented differently — for example, 'only fill with zero IF the prior 30 days have at least 25 non-null readings' — SOUTH would also change under the new platform-uniform fill-with-zero. The exercise specifies SOUTH's plain fill-with-zero, which exactly matches the new platform convention.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Identifies one of two changing teams correctly but invents a coincidence between EAST and the new policy that does not exist (forward-fill differs from zero-fill in the typical case, not the boundary case).

Option C: Invents a 'double-fill' arithmetic that does not occur — fill-with-zero is idempotent.

Option D: Over-claims by including SOUTH; SOUTH's numbers genuinely do not change since the conventions match.

fields.label: Q2 — Why is the Platform architect's framing — 'fill-with-zero is the most defensible default' — wrong as a consolidation argument, even if it might be right as a single-team convention?
options: Fill-with-zero is provably wrong as a default — it produces zero-consumption rows that the meter never reported, and any auditor reviewing such a row will reject it as fabricated data; no single-team convention should use fill-with-zero either

Defensibility of a default is a within-team property; consolidation across teams must preserve each team's audited contract, not replace them with a single convention chosen on its abstract merits without consulting the audited assumptions of each consuming team

The architect should have chosen forward-fill instead because it is the most defensible default among the three options under regulatory scrutiny; fill-with-zero is the second-best default, and drop-nulls is the worst because it changes row count

The architect should have polled the three teams via a vote and gone with the majority convention; the consolidation problem is fundamentally a coordination problem, not a technical one, and the Platform team's role is to facilitate not to decide
fields.correctAnswer: Defensibility of a default is a within-team property; consolidation across teams must preserve each team's audited contract, not replace them with a single convention chosen on its abstract merits without consulting the audited assumptions of each consuming team
fields.rationale:
The architect's argument is correct in the abstract: fill-with-zero IS a defensible default for many auditing contexts. The argument is incorrect as a consolidation move because it conflates 'defensible in the abstract' with 'preserves existing audited contracts'. Each of the three teams already passed their audit under a different convention. The consolidation must respect three already-binding contracts, not impose a fourth. The senior pattern: when N teams have N different audited contracts, consolidation does not mean choosing one of the N — it means designing a structure that lets each team's contract continue to be honored. Option A overreaches by claiming fill-with-zero is provably wrong (it is not — it is locally defensible for SOUTH's audit). Option C invents a regulatory hierarchy among the three conventions that does not exist. Option D's voting framework is process theater that does not address the contract problem.

When this answer is wrong: If none of the three teams had been previously audited under a specific convention, the architect's framing would be reasonable — picking the most defensible default would be the right move. The audit history is what changes the analysis, and the exercise specifies that all three teams are audited.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats fill-with-zero as wrong everywhere. It is wrong as a unilateral consolidation choice but correct in some single-team contexts (SOUTH's, here).

Option C: Invents a regulatory hierarchy that picks among the three conventions on abstract merit. Each convention is correct under its own regulator's audit.

Option D: Frames the issue as procedural — vote and pick a winner. Voting does not resolve the contract problem; it just transfers the violation to whichever side loses the vote.

fields.label: Q3 — What architectural pattern preserves all three teams' audited contracts while still enabling the platform-level consolidation goal (one source of truth for null-handled regional aggregates)?
options: Maintain three separate null-handled views over silver_meters — gold_regional_summary_drop_nulls, gold_regional_summary_fill_zero, gold_regional_summary_forward_fill — each owned by the Platform team but consumed by the team whose contract matches its convention

Make gold_regional_summary's null-handling a per-row column — store both the raw daily_kw with explicit nulls AND a daily_kw_filled column with the platform default — letting each team apply its own additional handling at read time atop the consolidated source

Have the Platform team expose silver_meters with a documented null-presence column (a boolean indicating whether the original reading was null) and require each consuming team to apply their own convention downstream, owned by the team whose contract demands it

Have the Platform team produce a single gold_regional_summary using the convention of the team with the strictest regulator (NORTH, since drop-nulls is the most conservative), and require the other two teams to renegotiate their audit contracts to match
fields.correctAnswer: Have the Platform team expose silver_meters with a documented null-presence column (a boolean indicating whether the original reading was null) and require each consuming team to apply their own convention downstream, owned by the team whose contract demands it
fields.rationale:
The consumer-side ownership pattern is the correct architectural answer. The Platform team's job is to provide the SHARED RAW MATERIAL — silver_meters with a null-presence column — and each team owns the convention-application step that produces their audited output. This decouples the platform's role (provide source of truth) from the consumers' role (apply consumer-specific contracts). It is the same pattern as DMS1.1's resolution, scaled from one consumer to three. Option A creates three Gold tables, putting the maintenance burden on the Platform team for three convention variants that the Platform team does not consume. Option B couples columns across teams (every team sees both raw and filled, expanding schema complexity). Option D is the unilateral consolidation that the question rejected upstream — picking one regulator's convention and asking the others to renegotiate.

When this answer is wrong: On a platform with very small consumer counts (e.g., one or two teams), option A's separate-views approach is sometimes simpler than per-team downstream logic. The exercise's three-team scope is large enough that the consumer-side pattern (option C) becomes the cleaner choice — and grows cleanly to N teams without N Gold tables.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Solves the problem by replication — three Gold variants. The platform owns three when it should own one source-of-truth.

Option B: Couples schema across teams. Every consumer pays the schema-complexity cost for every other consumer's convention.

Option D: Repeats the unilateral-consolidation move that Q2 already rejected, just from a different choice of convention.

fields.label: Q4 — The Platform team adopts the consumer-side ownership pattern (option C from Q3): silver_meters carries a null-presence column and each team applies their convention downstream. What new contract clause must the Platform team add to silver_meters to make this pattern sustainable across team membership changes?
options: A clause requiring every consumer to register their null-handling convention in writing as part of their pipeline metadata, so that future Platform team members can audit which conventions are in use without inspecting each team's pipeline source code

A clause requiring the Platform team to monitor null rates per region per day and alert when any region's null rate exceeds a baseline threshold, since null-rate changes affect the three teams' reports differently and surface as silent divergences across reports

A clause forbidding the Platform team from ever changing the null-presence column's semantics or removing it from the schema without a versioned contract change reviewed by all three Analytics teams, since the column is now load-bearing for all three consumers

A clause requiring a quarterly cross-team review where the three teams' downstream reports are compared against each other for explainable divergence, with the Platform team facilitating the comparison but not owning the conclusion
fields.correctAnswer: A clause forbidding the Platform team from ever changing the null-presence column's semantics or removing it from the schema without a versioned contract change reviewed by all three Analytics teams, since the column is now load-bearing for all three consumers
fields.rationale:
Once three teams' contracts depend on a column existing and meaning what it claims, that column has become structural infrastructure for those teams. The Platform team must commit not to remove or redefine the column without a versioned contract change. This is the same architectural commitment as DMS1.1's 'name the rule in the contract' but at the column-existence level rather than the rule level. The clause prevents a future Platform team member from cleaning up 'an unused-looking column' that is actually load-bearing. Option A's consumer-registration is useful but is a consumer responsibility, not a Platform-team contract clause — the question asks what the Platform team must commit to. Option B's null-rate monitoring is a useful defensive layer but does not address the column's contract status. Option D's quarterly review is process theater that does not commit to anything specific about the column.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms where columns are rarely removed and the team has strong institutional memory, option A's lighter-weight registration approach can be sufficient. The exercise's framing (turnover concern, future Platform members) makes the schema-stability commitment (option C) the binding constraint.

Operational note: When N teams have N distinct audited contracts on the same data, consolidation does not mean choosing one. The platform pattern is to provide raw material with a contract that names what is invariant (the schema, the null-presence column, the upstream-source identity), and require each consumer to own the convention-application step that produces their audited output. NordGrid adopted this pattern after a 2025 incident where a unilateral fill-with-zero consolidation broke two of three regional regulatory audits; the platform's data-contract template now includes a 'is this column load-bearing for downstream contracts?' question for every column proposed for the schema.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Places the obligation on consumers when the question is about the Platform team's commitment.

Option B: Adds monitoring layer instead of contract commitment. Monitoring detects; contract prevents.

Option D: Adds a process ritual without committing to the column's contract status.

tasks.title: Three-Pipeline Bronze-to-Silver Chain

description.context: NordGrid runs a three-pipeline chain across three teams. Pipeline A (owned by Platform) reads Bronze CSV nightly and writes raw_silver. Pipeline B (owned by Customer) reads raw_silver and writes customer_silver with customer enrichment from a customer-dimension table. Pipeline C (owned by Billing) reads customer_silver and writes billing_gold with revenue calculation joining a tariffs table. The Platform team is asked to introduce a defensive `dropna(subset=['daily_kw'])` step in Pipeline A — a small change motivated by a recent incident where downstream nulls caused intermittent spurious aggregates. The change is scheduled for next sprint. The Customer and Billing teams have not yet been formally consulted; the Platform team is treating the change as 'within Pipeline A's contract' because Pipeline A's documented contract says daily_kw is NULLABLE — and dropping nulls produces a stricter (subset) dataset.
description.task: Read the pipeline alongside the team context and ownership map — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.title: Production constraint
evidence.content: The Platform team's reasoning is technically defensible: a NULLABLE column producing fewer null rows is still satisfying its NULLABLE contract. But three downstream consumers have implicit assumptions about what 'fewer rows' means for their pipeline. The student must trace the consequences of the change across the three-pipeline chain and identify which downstream team is most at architectural risk.

evidence.title: Platform context
evidence.content: Platform team OWNS Pipeline A and raw_silver. Customer team OWNS Pipeline B and customer_silver; their pipeline does a merge with the customer-dimension table (left join on customer_id, expecting one customer_silver row to map to exactly one dimension row). Billing team OWNS Pipeline C and billing_gold; their pipeline does a merge with tariffs (range-join on customer_type and date, expecting one customer_silver row to produce zero or more billing_gold rows depending on tariff-version count). All three teams have separate test suites. No team has tests that consume another team's output as a fixture.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
# Pipeline A — Platform team — Bronze to raw_silver
# CURRENT: passes daily_kw nulls through
# PROPOSED: drops daily_kw null rows defensively
import pandas as pd

def pipeline_a():
    df = pd.read_csv('bronze/meters.csv', parse_dates=['date'])
    df['region'] = df['region'].str.strip().str.upper()
    # PROPOSED ADDITION (next sprint):
    # df = df.dropna(subset=['daily_kw'])
    df.to_parquet('raw_silver/meters.parquet')


# Pipeline B — Customer team — raw_silver to customer_silver
def pipeline_b():
    df = pd.read_parquet('raw_silver/meters.parquet')
    customers = pd.read_parquet('reference/customers_dim.parquet')
    # left merge — Customer team expects 1:1 cardinality between meters and customers
    df = df.merge(customers, on='customer_id', how='left')
    df.to_parquet('customer_silver/meters_enriched.parquet')


# Pipeline C — Billing team — customer_silver to billing_gold
def pipeline_c():
    df = pd.read_parquet('customer_silver/meters_enriched.parquet')
    tariffs = pd.read_parquet('reference/tariffs.parquet')
    # range-join on (customer_type, date in [valid_from, valid_to))
    df = df.merge(tariffs, on='customer_type', how='left')
    df = df[(df['date'] >= df['valid_from']) & (df['date'] < df['valid_to'])]
    df['revenue_eur'] = df['daily_kw'] * df['eur_per_kw']
    df = df.groupby(['region', 'customer_type', 'date'])['revenue_eur'].sum().reset_index()
    df.to_parquet('billing_gold/regional_revenue.parquet')

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: Pipeline A produces ~12M rows nightly; ~0.3% of rows have null daily_kw under normal conditions. Pipeline B's left merge is sized for the 12M-row input. Pipeline C's range-join produces ~12M rows after filter, then aggregates to ~120K rows in billing_gold. The Customer team's monthly customer-completeness report counts the number of distinct customer_ids in customer_silver vs the customer-dimension's full enumeration; deviation from 100% is investigated. The Billing team's monthly revenue-reconciliation compares billing_gold's per-region revenue total against the Finance team's bottom-up sum from the customer-dimension's predicted revenue per customer.

evidence.title: Ownership map
evidence.content: Platform → Pipeline A, raw_silver | Customer → Pipeline B, customer_silver | Billing → Pipeline C, billing_gold | (Implicit downstream) Finance → consumes billing_gold for monthly reconciliation

fields.label: Q1 — Which downstream pipeline notices the change first (in the sense of producing visibly different output)?
options: Pipeline B notices first — its customer-completeness report is the most direct mirror of upstream row count, so a 0.3% reduction in raw_silver produces an immediately visible 0.3% drop in distinct customer_ids appearing in customer_silver

Pipeline C notices first — its revenue-reconciliation report compares against the Finance team's bottom-up sum, and any per-region revenue drop (which the change produces by dropping null-daily_kw rows that contributed zero revenue anyway) appears in the next monthly reconciliation

Neither pipeline notices because the dropped rows had null daily_kw — null × any tariff is null in Pandas multiplication, so those rows never contributed to revenue, and the customer-completeness report counts customer_ids regardless of daily_kw nullness

Both pipelines notice on the same monthly report cycle — the upstream row-count drop propagates through both Pipeline B's distinct-id count and Pipeline C's per-region revenue, and both teams investigate the discrepancy on the same monthly cadence
fields.correctAnswer: Pipeline B notices first — its customer-completeness report is the most direct mirror of upstream row count, so a 0.3% reduction in raw_silver produces an immediately visible 0.3% drop in distinct customer_ids appearing in customer_silver
fields.rationale:
Visibility kicks in first downstream of Pipeline B, where the Customer team's monthly metric tracks per-day customer enumeration against the dimension's full population. When raw_silver loses 0.3% of rows (the null-daily_kw rows), some customer_ids will appear in fewer or zero rows. If a customer's only nightly reading happened to be null on a given day, that customer_id might not appear in customer_silver at all that day. The Customer team's distinct-id count drops by some fraction of the 0.3%. The drop is visible on the next monthly report cycle. Pipeline C's revenue does NOT change in the way option B claims — null daily_kw multiplied by any tariff produces null, which the groupby sum drops, so those rows contribute zero to revenue both before and after the change. Option C is correct that revenue is unaffected but wrong that NEITHER pipeline notices — Pipeline B's completeness count IS affected. Option D over-claims that both pipelines see the change.

When this answer is wrong: If the Customer team's completeness report counted distinct customer_ids over a longer time window (e.g., monthly distinct, not daily distinct), the dropped rows would be amortized across the month and the visible effect would shrink to near-zero. The exercise frames the report at daily granularity rolled up monthly, which is the case where the row-drop produces visible signal.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Believes Pipeline C's revenue changes. Null × tariff = null, dropped by sum — revenue is unchanged.

Option C: Identifies revenue invariance correctly but misses Pipeline B's completeness count.

Option D: Over-claims both pipelines see the change; Pipeline C does not.

fields.label: Q2 — Which downstream pipeline is most at architectural risk from the change?
options: Pipeline B — the Customer team's customer-completeness report relies on raw_silver's row count being a faithful representation of the upstream meter population; any upstream row-count change without team coordination causes their completeness metric to drift in ways the team cannot easily explain to their auditor

Pipeline C — the Billing team's range-join on tariffs has a one-to-many cardinality on the right side; the dropped rows would have produced zero or more billing_gold rows depending on tariff-version count, and the cardinality interaction makes the Billing team's reconciliation harder to align with Finance

Pipeline A — the Platform team's defensive change introduces an obligation to maintain the dropna step indefinitely, since downstream consumers will silently start to depend on the post-drop behavior even though the contract still says NULLABLE; the architectural risk is in the team that owns the change

Pipeline B AND Pipeline C share equal architectural risk — both depend on raw_silver's row population, both produce monthly reports against external benchmarks, and both have no test fixtures consuming the previous-pipeline's output, so neither team has an automated way to detect the change's effects
fields.correctAnswer: Pipeline B — the Customer team's customer-completeness report relies on raw_silver's row count being a faithful representation of the upstream meter population; any upstream row-count change without team coordination causes their completeness metric to drift in ways the team cannot easily explain to their auditor
fields.rationale:
Pipeline B carries the most architectural exposure. Its primary monthly metric (customer-completeness) directly relies on a property the contract does not guarantee — that the row population in raw_silver mirrors the meter population. The Platform team's defensive dropna does not violate the schema-level contract (NULLABLE column, fewer non-null values is still NULLABLE) but it violates the implicit contract that the Customer team's report relied on. The Customer team's auditor will ask why the completeness number drifted, and the team will not have a clean explanation because they did not own the change. Pipeline C is at less risk because its revenue is unaffected (null × tariff = null is dropped by sum). Pipeline A's risk (option C) is real but secondary — the maintenance commitment is real, but it does not affect downstream contracts directly. Option D's symmetric framing misses that Pipeline C's revenue output is mathematically robust to null-row drops in this specific case.

When this answer is wrong: If the Customer team's report were defined differently (e.g., 'count meter_ids' instead of 'count distinct customer_ids'), the upstream row-drop's visibility shifts and a different team's risk profile dominates. The exercise's framing — distinct customer_id completeness — makes Pipeline B's risk the dominant one.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option B: Identifies a real cardinality concern but the math (null × tariff = null) makes the revenue invariant; the cardinality interaction does not surface in this specific scenario.

Option C: Identifies a real maintenance burden on the change-owning team but treats it as architectural risk on consumers — those are different concerns.

Option D: Treats both downstream pipelines symmetrically; Pipeline C is mathematically robust to this specific change.

fields.label: Q3 — Which pipeline's tests would actually catch the change before it ships to production, given the current test architecture?
options: Pipeline A's tests — the Platform team's CI runs the new dropna step on a representative sample, and a row-count assertion would catch the change relative to the previous-version's expected row count, alerting the Platform team before production deploy

Pipeline B's tests — the Customer team's CI consumes a fixture mimicking raw_silver, and the dropna change would manifest as a fixture-row-count mismatch when the next CI run pulls the latest raw_silver schema definition from the Platform team's repo

Pipeline C's tests — the Billing team's CI computes revenue against a known fixture and compares against an expected total; the change would propagate through Pipeline B's transformations into Pipeline C's input fixture and produce a different revenue total

None of the three pipelines' tests catch the change — Pipeline A's tests check the schema only, and the dropna step is consistent with the schema; Pipeline B and C have no test fixtures consuming real upstream output, so the change manifests only at production runtime
fields.correctAnswer: None of the three pipelines' tests catch the change — Pipeline A's tests check the schema only, and the dropna step is consistent with the schema; Pipeline B and C have no test fixtures consuming real upstream output, so the change manifests only at production runtime
fields.rationale:
All three test suites miss the change. Pipeline A's tests verify that Pipeline A produces the schema-compliant output it claims to produce — they cannot flag a change that is itself within the stated schema (NULLABLE column, dropna produces a stricter NULLABLE column, still schema-compliant). Pipeline B's tests use a fixture that mimics raw_silver but is a static fixture, not the live raw_silver; the fixture does not change when Pipeline A changes. Pipeline C's tests similarly use a static customer_silver fixture. The only way the change surfaces is in production monthly reports. This is the architectural gap: cross-team test integration. Option A is wrong because Pipeline A's row-count assertion (if any) would compare to the post-change expected row count — the change is the dev's intent, not a regression. Option B and C invent test fixtures that automatically refresh from the upstream's repo, which is not the actual test architecture described.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms that DO have cross-team integration tests (e.g., a nightly job that runs each pipeline against the previous pipeline's actual output and compares against fixtures), Pipeline B's or C's tests might catch the change. NordGrid's stated test architecture (per the team_context) does not have this — making option D the right diagnosis.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats the change as a regression rather than as an intentional change. Pipeline A's tests pass because the change is what the dev intended.

Option B: Invents an auto-refreshing fixture pattern that the team_context says does not exist.

Option C: Same as B but for Pipeline C — invents test infrastructure that is not present.

fields.label: Q4 — What contract clause should be added to raw_silver's contract to prevent the next change of this type from going unnoticed?
options: Add a 'row-count stability' clause requiring the Platform team to publish the daily row-count delta and notify all consumers when the delta exceeds a documented threshold (e.g., 0.1%) so consumers can investigate before their monthly reports surface the discrepancy

Add a 'defensive-step registry' clause requiring any defensive cleaning step (dropna, fillna, drop_duplicates, rename) added to a Bronze-to-Silver pipeline to be documented in a registry that downstream consumers can subscribe to for change notifications

Promote the upstream row-count to a contract guarantee — the contract states 'raw_silver contains all rows from Bronze that pass schema validation, with no defensive filtering' — making any future dropna step a versioned contract change requiring explicit consumer review

Add a 'consumer-list' clause requiring the Platform team to maintain a registry of all downstream consumers with their read-pattern and contact information, and to consult the registry before any pipeline change however small
fields.correctAnswer: Promote the upstream row-count to a contract guarantee — the contract states 'raw_silver contains all rows from Bronze that pass schema validation, with no defensive filtering' — making any future dropna step a versioned contract change requiring explicit consumer review
fields.rationale:
Lifting the row-count behavior into a stated guarantee is the architecturally correct fix. The current contract describes the schema (column types, NULLABLE) but says nothing about row-count semantics. Adding a clause like 'raw_silver contains all rows from Bronze that pass schema validation, with no defensive filtering' makes any future dropna a versioned contract change rather than an internal optimization. This is the same pattern as DMS1.1's 'name the rule in the contract' — promoting an implicit property to an explicit contract clause. Option A's threshold-based notification helps detect drift after it ships but does not prevent the silent change. Option B's defensive-step registry is process layered atop the contract — it tracks WHAT was added but does not commit the team to NOT add. Option D's consumer-list clause is heavyweight (the Platform team must consult every consumer for every change however small) and impractical at scale.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms where the upstream raw layer genuinely needs defensive cleaning for downstream safety (e.g., a Bronze layer producing malformed data that downstream pipelines cannot tolerate), the row-count guarantee in option C becomes infeasible — the platform must filter for safety. The exercise specifies the dropna is defensive (the previous incident was caused by downstream nulls, not by raw_silver malformedness), making the upstream-fidelity contract the right answer.

Operational note: Bronze-to-Silver pipelines often add defensive cleaning steps for legitimate reasons (a recent incident, an upstream malformedness, a downstream complaint). The architectural risk is that the cleaning step alters the row-count semantics that downstream pipelines have implicitly come to depend on. The platform-level pattern is to make row-count behavior explicit in the contract — either as a guarantee ('all schema-valid rows pass through') or as a documented filter set ('the following defensive filters are applied: ...'). NordGrid's data-contract template adopted this pattern after a 2025 incident with the same shape as this exercise.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Uses notification as a substitute for contract. Notification surfaces post-hoc; contract prevents up-front.

Option B: Treats the change as a registration matter rather than as a contract matter.

Option D: Over-engineers consultation for every change. The contract clause makes the consultation requirement specific to changes that violate the clause.

tasks.crossReference: Foundations curriculum: Senior chapter, 'Multi-team pipeline architecture' section. PSS-equivalent senior modules: PSS5 lessons 3-4 (Data Contracts and row-count semantics); the same incident class appears in PySpark Aggregations Senior JAS5.1 (the 4-hour Tuesday-to-Wednesday regression — different mechanism but same root cause: implicit upstream contract).

tasks.title: The Reference Table That Two Teams Own

description.context: NordGrid maintains two reference tables that overlap on the 'region' attribute. The Customer team owns customers_dim — a customer-master dimension keyed by customer_id, with columns (customer_id, customer_name, customer_type, region). The Substations team owns substations_dim — a substation-master dimension keyed by substation_id, with columns (substation_id, substation_name, region, capacity_mw). Both tables include 'region' as a denormalized attribute. The Customer team uses the encoding 'NORTH'/'SOUTH'/'EAST'/'WEST'. The Substations team uses the encoding 'N'/'S'/'E'/'W'. A downstream Analytics pipeline reads both tables and joins them via meter readings to produce per-region aggregates — relying on an unstated assumption that the two tables agree on 'region'. The Analytics team has been emitting a quarterly per-region capacity-utilization report under this assumption for two years. A junior engineer on the Analytics team notices the mismatch while debugging an unrelated issue.
description.task: Read the pipeline alongside the team context and ownership map — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.title: Production constraint
evidence.content: Two reference tables, two owners, one shared attribute, no contract specifying who owns the encoding. The Analytics report has been silently incorrect for two years — capacity utilization per region has been computed against substation totals (which use 'N') joined to meter populations (which use 'NORTH'). The mismatch produces empty join results for the cross-tier rows, suppressing some capacity from the utilization denominator and inflating utilization percentages. The student must reason about which team should own the encoding going forward and what platform-level commitments prevent the next instance.

evidence.title: Platform context
evidence.content: Customer team OWNS customers_dim. Substations team OWNS substations_dim. Analytics team OWNS the per-region capacity-utilization report. Platform team OWNS the Bronze-to-Silver pipeline that produces meter readings (which carry their own region column, encoded as 'NORTH' to match the Customer team's convention — the Platform team adopted the Customer convention three years ago without documenting the decision). No central enumeration table exists. The Substations team's 'N'/'S'/'E'/'W' encoding predates the Customer team's 'NORTH' convention by five years; the Substations team has never been asked to change.

evidence.label: pipeline.py
evidence.content:
# Analytics team's quarterly capacity-utilization report
import pandas as pd

def capacity_utilization_quarterly(quarter_end):
    meters = pd.read_parquet('silver/meters.parquet')
    customers = pd.read_parquet('reference/customers_dim.parquet')   # Customer team
    substations = pd.read_parquet('reference/substations_dim.parquet')  # Substations team

    # Join meters to customers on customer_id
    df = meters.merge(customers[['customer_id', 'customer_type', 'region']],
                      on='customer_id', how='left',
                      suffixes=('', '_cust'))

    # Join the result to substations on (substation_id, region)
    # The Analytics team's intent: join on substation_id alone would suffice,
    # but they include region as a 'sanity check' to ensure the join makes sense
    df = df.merge(substations[['substation_id', 'region', 'capacity_mw']],
                  on=['substation_id', 'region'], how='left',
                  suffixes=('', '_sub'))

    # Per-region aggregation
    return df.groupby('region').agg(
        total_consumption=('daily_kw', 'sum'),
        total_capacity=('capacity_mw', 'sum'),
        utilization=('daily_kw', lambda x: x.sum() / df.loc[x.index, 'capacity_mw'].sum())
    ).reset_index()

# Sample customers_dim: customer_id=C-001, customer_name='Acme', customer_type='COMMERCIAL', region='NORTH'
# Sample substations_dim: substation_id=S-001, substation_name='Vilnius-1', region='N', capacity_mw=50
# Sample meter:           customer_id=C-001, substation_id=S-001, daily_kw=120, region='NORTH'

# When the second merge runs with on=['substation_id','region'], 'NORTH' fails to match 'N'
# and capacity_mw comes back null for that row. The utilization metric silently understates
# total_capacity, inflating the utilization ratio.

evidence.title: Team context
evidence.content: The Customer team has had its 'NORTH'/'SOUTH'/'EAST'/'WEST' convention since the company's first data warehouse; it matches the company's official region names. The Substations team has used 'N'/'S'/'E'/'W' since before the data warehouse existed — the encoding came from the legacy SCADA system that exposes substation metadata. Both teams' downstream consumers (excluding Analytics) work with one table or the other in isolation; the divergence does not surface inside either team's own pipelines. The Analytics report's incorrect output has not been audited externally; the regulator does not look at capacity-utilization at this granularity.

evidence.title: Ownership map
evidence.content: Customer → customers_dim (and the 'NORTH' encoding within it) | Substations → substations_dim (and the 'N' encoding within it) | Platform → meters silver (using 'NORTH' inherited from Customer team's convention, undocumented) | Analytics → quarterly capacity-utilization report (consuming both reference tables and meters)

fields.label: Q1 — Which team should own the platform-level region encoding going forward?
options: The Customer team — their 'NORTH'/'SOUTH'/'EAST'/'WEST' convention matches the company's official region names, is already used by the Platform team's Silver layer, and is the only encoding that aligns with external regulatory and contract documents that reference NordGrid's regions

The Substations team — their 'N'/'S'/'E'/'W' encoding has the longer history (predating the data warehouse by 5 years), originates from the SCADA system that is the closest physical-world ground truth, and is shorter so it joins more efficiently in the storage layer

Neither team — region encoding should be lifted out of both reference tables into a third dedicated 'regions_dim' table owned by the Platform team, with both customers_dim and substations_dim referencing it via a region_id foreign key rather than carrying the string code denormalized

Both teams jointly — region encoding is a cross-cutting concern that both teams genuinely share, so neither team should be sole owner; a joint working group with representatives from both teams maintains the canonical mapping and resolves conflicts when they arise
fields.correctAnswer: Neither team — region encoding should be lifted out of both reference tables into a third dedicated 'regions_dim' table owned by the Platform team, with both customers_dim and substations_dim referencing it via a region_id foreign key rather than carrying the string code denormalized
fields.rationale:
Region as an attribute of customer-master and substation-master is conceptually a separate entity (a region is neither a property of a customer nor a property of a substation — it's a geographic/operational unit that both reference). The architecturally correct move is to extract it into its own dimension owned by the Platform team and have both customers_dim and substations_dim reference it via a foreign key. This prevents the two-owners-one-attribute pathology that the exercise illustrates, removes the ambiguity about who decides the encoding, and makes the encoding centrally evolvable. Option A picks the Customer team based on tactical alignment (matching what's already in use elsewhere) but leaves the Substations team's table still carrying region as a denormalized string — only postponing the next divergence. Option B picks the Substations team based on history; the same critique applies. Option D's joint ownership is a process answer to a structural problem and tends to fail under team-membership turnover.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms with extreme storage-cost sensitivity where joins to a region table would meaningfully harm query performance, denormalizing region into both tables (option A or B) becomes pragmatic. The exercise targets a typical analytical workload where the join cost is negligible and the structural correctness of option C dominates.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Picks an owner among the two existing teams instead of recognizing the structural fix. Tactically reasonable but architecturally postpones the problem.

Option B: Same as A but with a different tactical choice. The 'longer history' argument is irrelevant once the problem is structurally resolved.

Option D: Process answer to a structural problem. Joint ownership without structural separation is fragile across team-membership changes.

fields.label: Q2 — What contract should exist between the Customer team and the Substations team to prevent this class of divergence?
options: A bilateral encoding-agreement contract — the two teams agree in writing on a single canonical region encoding, register the agreement in a shared platform-team-maintained registry, and treat any change to the encoding as a versioned contract change requiring both teams' sign-off

A schema-naming contract — each team renames their region column distinctively (e.g., customer_region, substation_region) so that downstream consumers cannot accidentally join on them without explicit awareness of the encoding difference, surfacing the issue at the join-write time

A consumer-notification contract — both teams publish their region encoding to a platform-team-maintained registry, and any downstream consumer joining the two must register their join intent so the platform team can flag known-incompatible encodings before the join ships to production

No bilateral contract is needed — the architecturally correct fix is option C from Q1 (lift region into a third table), which makes the bilateral contract structurally unnecessary because the two teams stop having a shared concern at the column level
fields.correctAnswer: No bilateral contract is needed — the architecturally correct fix is option C from Q1 (lift region into a third table), which makes the bilateral contract structurally unnecessary because the two teams stop having a shared concern at the column level
fields.rationale:
Once Q1's answer ships and region lives in a separate dimension, the customer-substation coordination problem ceases to exist at the attribute level — both source tables now reference the encoding via foreign key rather than carrying it. The bilateral coordination problem disappears. Option A (bilateral encoding-agreement) is what the teams should do TEMPORARILY before the structural fix lands — but it is a stopgap, not the long-term contract. Option B (rename the columns) hides the problem from accidental joins but does not solve the inconsistency; teams who need to join intentionally still face the encoding mismatch. Option C (consumer-notification registry) is heavyweight and does not address the source of the inconsistency. The senior pattern: when a contract is needed to coordinate two teams on an attribute they share, examine whether the attribute should be lifted out of both their tables. Often the answer is yes, and the contract becomes unnecessary.

When this answer is wrong: If the structural fix from Q1 is judged too costly (e.g., the storage-cost sensitivity case mentioned above), then the bilateral encoding-agreement (option A) becomes the right answer — the contract substitutes for the structure that was not lifted. The exercise's framing assumes the structural fix is feasible.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Treats the bilateral contract as the long-term solution rather than as a stopgap.

Option B: Hides the problem rather than solving it.

Option C: Adds platform-level overhead without addressing the source.

fields.label: Q3 — What platform-level monitoring would have surfaced the divergence to the Analytics team within days of the report's first run, rather than two years later?
options: Schema-fingerprint monitoring on both reference tables — comparing the distinct-value sets in 'region' across customers_dim and substations_dim daily, alerting when the two sets do not match exactly; this would have flagged 'NORTH' vs 'N' as a divergence on day one

Join-result monitoring on the Analytics report's pipeline — flagging when a left-join produces an unexpectedly high null rate on the right-side columns, indicating the join is not finding matches; this would have surfaced as 100% null capacity_mw in cross-tier rows

Aggregate-output monitoring on the report itself — comparing the report's total_capacity sum to a known-truth total from the Substations team's capacity inventory, flagging when the report's sum is meaningfully smaller than the inventory's

All three monitors would have caught it independently — schema-fingerprint catches the encoding mismatch at the source, join-result catches the symptom at the consumer, aggregate-output catches the impact at the report; defense-in-depth requires all three
fields.correctAnswer: Join-result monitoring on the Analytics report's pipeline — flagging when a left-join produces an unexpectedly high null rate on the right-side columns, indicating the join is not finding matches; this would have surfaced as 100% null capacity_mw in cross-tier rows
fields.rationale:
Join-result monitoring is the highest-signal of the three. The Analytics report's pipeline does a left-join on (substation_id, region); when the encoding divergence causes the join to fail on the region component, the right-side columns come back null. A monitor that asserts the null rate on capacity_mw is below some threshold (e.g., <1%) would have alerted on day one of the report — capacity_mw was 100% null for any row where the encoding did not match. Option A (schema-fingerprint on both reference tables) would also catch it but assumes the platform team monitors all reference tables proactively, which is less likely than each consumer monitoring its own joins. Option C (aggregate-output vs known-truth) is harder to operationalize because the 'known truth' is itself derived from the same Substations data, making the comparison circular. Option D over-prescribes — defense-in-depth is always better but the question asked which monitor would have surfaced the divergence soonest, and join-result is the most direct.

When this answer is wrong: On platforms where reference-table monitoring is centralized and well-instrumented (e.g., the Platform team runs schema-fingerprint comparisons on every reference table nightly), option A becomes the more reliable answer because it catches the divergence at the source rather than at the consumer. The exercise's framing (no central reference-table monitoring is described) makes consumer-side join monitoring the most likely to fire first.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Assumes proactive central monitoring of all reference tables, which most platforms do not maintain.

Option C: Requires a known-truth that is itself derived from the same data, making the comparison vulnerable to the same divergence.

Option D: Over-prescribes defense-in-depth when the question asks which single monitor would have caught it soonest.

fields.label: Q4 — Which downstream pipeline (other than the Analytics report) is most exposed to this divergence?
options: The Customer team's billing pipeline — it joins customers_dim with meter readings on customer_id, and the divergence in region encoding affects the per-region revenue aggregation that the billing pipeline emits, producing incorrect regional revenue totals

The Substations team's capacity-planning pipeline — it joins substations_dim with substation-event logs, and any inconsistency in region encoding propagates into the regional capacity-planning forecasts that drive infrastructure investment decisions

The Platform team's Bronze-to-Silver pipeline — it joins meter readings with both reference tables to enrich the Silver layer with region information, and the encoding divergence produces inconsistent regional tagging on Silver rows depending on which reference table the join hit

No other consumer is currently affected — each existing pipeline reads only one of the two source tables, so the encoding mismatch stays invisible inside their workloads; the bug surfaces only when a single workload joins both, and today only one such workload exists
fields.correctAnswer: No other consumer is currently affected — each existing pipeline reads only one of the two source tables, so the encoding mismatch stays invisible inside their workloads; the bug surfaces only when a single workload joins both, and today only one such workload exists
fields.rationale:
Looking across the platform, only one workload combines both reference tables — the quarterly capacity-utilization report. Every other consumer of either table works from a single source and cannot encounter the encoding mismatch. Other pipelines that read only customers_dim see only the 'NORTH' encoding internally consistent; pipelines that read only substations_dim see only 'N' internally consistent. The pathology requires joining both tables on the region attribute, and the Analytics report is the only pipeline doing so. Option A invents a billing pipeline behavior (per-region revenue from customer_dim joins) that does not depend on the substations encoding. Option B invents a substations capacity-planning pipeline that depends on cross-table region consistency, which the team_context rules out. Option C invents a Platform team Bronze-to-Silver pipeline that joins both reference tables, which contradicts the team_context.

When this answer is wrong: If a new consumer is being designed that needs both reference tables — for example, a future customer-substation-spatial-affinity report — that consumer would inherit the same exposure as the Analytics report. The exercise's question is about CURRENTLY exposed pipelines, where Analytics is unique.

Operational note: When two teams own reference tables that carry the same denormalized attribute, the long-term architectural fix is to lift that attribute into its own dimension owned by a neutral team. The bilateral encoding-agreement is a useful stopgap but tends to fragment under team-membership turnover. NordGrid's data-platform team adopted a 'shared-attribute review' process in 2026 Q1 in which any new dimension table must declare which of its denormalized attributes are also present in OTHER dimension tables, with the platform team responsible for evaluating whether to lift each shared attribute into its own dimension.
fields.distractorRationale:
Option A: Invents cross-table behavior in the billing pipeline that the team_context rules out.

Option B: Invents cross-table behavior in the capacity-planning pipeline that the team_context rules out.

Option C: Invents cross-table behavior in the Platform team's pipeline that the team_context rules out.

tasks.crossReference: Foundations curriculum: Senior chapter, 'Shared-attribute architecture across reference tables' section. PSS-equivalent senior modules: PSS6 lessons on dimension modeling and shared-attribute extraction; PySpark Aggregations Senior JAS3.2 (the cross-country join key incident is the same architectural class — two upstream sources, divergent encoding, downstream join failure).

tasks.title: Pandas → Polars On The Mid-Sized Team

description.context: A senior engineer on the NordGrid Customer team has authored a migration proposal: replace the team's 18 Pandas pipelines with Polars equivalents. The proposal cites a Polars benchmark showing 8× speedup on representative workloads — peak runtime would drop from 45 minutes to roughly 5–6 minutes. The proposal is well-written and technically literate. It does NOT, however, address the team's actual operational profile: which engineers can debug Polars at 2am during an incident, what the team's schema-evolution cadence is, or what happens to the 12 downstream Pandas consumer pipelines that read the team's Silver output. The proposal goes to the team lead for approval. The team lead asks a senior architect (the student) to review.
description.task: Read the migration proposal in the context of the team workload and operational constraints — then answer all the questions below.

evidence.title: Production constraint
evidence.content: The proposal is technically correct about the speedup. The senior architect's job is to identify which production constraint the proposal MISSES that would settle the decision — not to evaluate the headline benefit but to find the binding constraint that the proposal failed to address.

evidence.title: Platform context
evidence.content: Customer team: 4 engineers maintaining 18 Pandas pipelines, total daily volume 30M rows, peak runtime 45 minutes during the 2-hour Bronze-to-Silver window. On-call rotation: 4 engineers, weekly. Team-skill profile: 1 engineer wrote Polars at a previous company; 3 engineers have never touched Polars. Schema-evolution cadence: the team ships breaking schema changes to its Silver layer every 2 weeks (driven by Bronze upstream changes), each requiring all 18 pipelines to be updated. Downstream consumer set: 12 pipelines across 4 other teams (Billing, Analytics-NORTH, Analytics-SOUTH, Substations) — all written in Pandas, all consuming the Customer team's Silver output. The team lead's calendar shows three days available for review before approving or rejecting the proposal.

evidence.title: Migration proposal
evidence.content:
MIGRATION PROPOSAL: Customer team Pandas → Polars

MOTIVATION:
  Polars benchmark on our representative workload shows 8× speedup. Our peak nightly runtime is 45 minutes; post-migration it would be ~5-6 minutes, freeing 40 minutes of cluster time during the Bronze-to-Silver window.

SCOPE:
  All 18 of the team's pipelines, migrated in three batches over six weeks.

APPROACH:
  - Batch 1 (week 1-2): the 6 simplest pipelines (read CSV, light transformations, write Parquet) — author proves migration pattern
  - Batch 2 (week 3-4): the 8 medium-complexity pipelines (joins, aggregations, multi-step chains)
  - Batch 3 (week 5-6): the 4 heaviest pipelines (the ones currently running 30+ minutes individually)

RISKS:
  - Polars and Pandas have minor semantic differences on edge cases. Migration tests will compare row-by-row output between old and new.
  - If a pipeline produces a meaningful divergence, we hold the migration of that specific pipeline and investigate.

AUTHOR: <senior engineer> · 2026-04-15 · v1.2

evidence.title: Team workload
evidence.content:
Representative pipeline shapes from the team's 18 pipelines:
  - 8 are simple cleaning chains (read CSV, dropna/fillna/cast, write Parquet) — typical 8 minutes runtime
  - 6 are join-heavy (merge with reference tables, then aggregate) — typical 15 minutes
  - 4 are heavy aggregation + ranking + filter (the 30+ minute pipelines)
  Schema across all 18: similar shape (date, region, customer_id, daily_kw, customer_type, substation_id), differences in extra enriched columns.

evidence.title: Operational context
evidence.content:
On-call: 4 engineers, weekly rotation. Last-quarter incident frequency: 2 production incidents per month requiring after-hours pipeline debugging.
Polars experience by engineer:
  - Engineer A (proposal author): 2 years Polars at previous company; recent
  - Engineer B: never used Polars; willing to learn
  - Engineer C: never used Polars; ambivalent
  - Engineer D: never used Polars; on parental leave for the next 4 months (returns mid-batch-2 of the proposed migration)
Schema-evolution cadence: Bronze upstream ships breaking changes every ~2 weeks. Each breaking change requires all 18 pipelines to be patched within the same week.
Downstream consumers: 12 Pandas pipelines across 4 teams. The Customer team's Silver Parquet output is consumed by all 12 — Polars and Pandas both read Parquet, so the file format does not change, but column dtype defaults differ slightly (e.g., Polars' Utf8 vs Pandas' object/string). One Billing pipeline has been documented to fail on Utf8 columns in some Pandas versions.

fields.label: Q1 — Which of the following is the BINDING constraint the proposal failed to address — the one that should settle the decision?
options: On-call coverage during the migration: 3 of 4 engineers have never written Polars, and Engineer D is on parental leave for the next 4 months — meaning during weeks 1-16 of migration plus stabilization, only 1 engineer can confidently debug a Polars production incident at 2am, which is a single-point-of-failure for the team's on-call rotation

The 8× speedup benchmark may not hold on the team's actual workload — benchmarks are typically authored on synthetic data, and real production pipelines often see 2-3× speedup rather than 8×, which would not be enough to justify the migration cost given the schema-evolution cadence

Polars' immature ecosystem of testing tools and IDE support makes the migration cost higher than the proposal estimates — the team would lose roughly 30% of their development velocity for the duration of the migration, and the proposal does not budget for that velocity loss

The 12 downstream Pandas consumers will silently fail when the Customer team's Silver Parquet output starts emitting Polars-default Utf8 columns — the Billing team's pipeline is documented as failing on Utf8 in some Pandas versions, which is a hard production-impact risk that the proposal mentions but does not actually address
